title: Rydberg state engineering of trapped ions
authors: Robin Thomm, Vinay Shankar, Natalia Kuk, Marion Mallweger, Ivo Straka, Markus Hennrich
abstract: Microwave dressing of Rydberg ions creates tunable eigenstates with controllable polarizability and interaction strength, but coherent navigation between these states has remained elusive. Here, we report on the first demonstration of coherent population transfer between different Rydberg states of a trapped ion. We investigate both microwave-mediated Rabi oscillations between Rydberg S and P states and adiabatic transfer between microwave-dressed Rydberg states. Between Rydberg S and P states we achieve a population transfer efficiency of 91.5(5)% in a single microwave $π$-pulse. Microwave dressing hybridizes the S and P Rydberg states into new eigenstates with tunable polarizability, enabling both noise-resilient zero-polarizability states and maximally interacting states. We demonstrate adiabatic transfer between these zero-polarizability and maximally dressed states, enabling experiments that combine noise-resilient excitation with strong dipole-dipole interactions and Förster resonance control within a single measurement sequence.
summary: This paper reports the first demonstration of coherent population transfer between multiple Rydberg states in a trapped ion. By using microwave dressing, the researchers engineered new eigenstates with tunable properties, allowing for both noise-resilient excitation and strong dipole-dipole interactions in a single sequence. This significantly expands the toolbox for quantum simulation and quantum information processing using trapped ions.
main_problem: The primary goal was to demonstrate coherent population transfer between different Rydberg states of a trapped ion, overcoming previous limitations of using only a single state per experiment.
main_result: The authors achieved a high population transfer efficiency (91.5%) in a single microwave $\pi$-pulse and demonstrated adiabatic transfer between engineered zero-polarizability and maximally interacting dressed states.
method: The work combines microwave dressing/driving with precise laser control to manipulate the quantum states of trapped ions, probing both Rabi oscillations and adiabatic passage.
model_or_system: The physical system is a trapped $^{88}	ext{Sr}^+$ ion. The dynamics are modeled using the Lindblad master equation, describing interactions between bare Rydberg states ($rS, rP$) and engineered dressed states ($|+angle, |-angle$) via microwave coupling.
key_observables: Population transfer efficiency, Rabi oscillation frequencies ($\Omega_{MW}$), and the fidelity of adiabatic passage.
important_parameters: Rydberg principal quantum number ($n=46$), microwave detuning ($\Delta_{MW}$), and state lifetimes ($	au_{rS} = 8.2 \mu s$).
main_assumptions: The primary limitations noted are Rydberg state dephasing, blackbody radiation induced decay, and non-adiabatic loss mechanisms during sweeping.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the experimental setup, Rabi oscillations between D and $rS$, the pulse sequence for state manipulation, and the dependence of adiabatic transfer efficiency on the microwave detuning.
paper_structure: The paper details the experimental setup, first demonstrating Rabi oscillations between bare states, then introducing microwave dressing to create tunable eigenstates, and finally demonstrating adiabatic transfer between these engineered dressed states.
why_interesting: This work provides a robust, controllable platform for quantum simulation by enabling fast, coherent switching between states with vastly different polarizabilities and interaction strengths, crucial for advanced quantum simulation protocols.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 2. a) Simplified level scheme (see the main text for details), with the main transitions indicated by arrows (repump transitions from metastable states are not shown for clarity). All shown excited S and P states have a lifetime of below 100 µs and ultimately decay to the ground state S (or a metastable state that is repumped to S ). Excitation to one of these states followed by decay can therefore be distinguished from population remaining in the metastable state D by fluorescence detection. b) Rabi oscillations between the D and rS state for n = 46. We measure the population in D (detected as no fluorescence = dark) after a two-photon Rydberg excitation pulse; population in other...

FIG. 1. Sketch of the experimental setup. Shown are all lasers needed for Rydberg excitation and manipulation as well as the imaging path. The first Rydberg excitation laser (243 nm) is overlapped with the MW radiation, the second one (305 nm) addresses the ion antiparallel to the first one to reduce Doppler broadening of the two-photon reso- nance and is overlapped with the imaging path (422 nm). Not shown are the qubit, Doppler cooling, and repumping lasers, which address the ion from the bottom. Furthermore, fluorescence light is collected from above and detected with a photomultiplier tube (PMT).

FIG. 3. a) Pulse sequence used for exciting and manipulating the Rydberg state. We start with an ion initialized in its electronic and motional ground state. A qubit π-pulse excites it into the metastable D state. From there we excite the ion via a two-photon process to the Rydberg state (cf. Fig. 2). Once in the Rydberg manifold, we coherently transfer population between different Rydberg states via MW radiation. We probe the success of the coherent manipulation by driving the fraction remaining in the Rydberg rS state back to D with a two-photon de-excitation pulse, all other population decays to S . The experiment is concluded with a fluorescence measurement. b) MW Rabi oscillations...

FIG. 4. Experimental sequence for probing the adiabatic transfer between different MW-dressed Rydberg states. In a) the pulse sequence is shown, in b) the dressed state energies dependent on the MW detuning ∆MW. We again start by transferring population to D. The Rydberg excitation and manipulation is then divided into four periods. In periods 1 and 4 both Rydberg lasers are switched simultaneously to transfer population between D and |+⟩0pol. In periods 2 and 3 the MW detuning can be swept (see the text for a more detailed description of the periods). During the whole time of periods 1 to 4, the MW irradiates the ion with variable detunings. We investigate 3 different cases, a constant...

FIG. 5. MW generation for both principal quantum numbers n = 46 (top) and n = 56 (bottom). A seed signal is switched before being quadrupled in frequency and amplified in a signal generator exten- sion (SGX) module (azure background). For n = 46, the signal, in the WR15 frequency range, is once more frequency doubled, result- ing in a signal in the WR8 frequency range. For n = 56, the signal after the SGX is upconverted with an RF signal of (200 −400) MHz. In both cases, the signal is coupled to free space with a conical horn antenna. Single lines indicate coaxial connections; double lines indi- cate waveguides.
